preamble: The shift from the alveolar fricative [s] to a retroflex stop like [ʈ] involves both a change in the place of articulation (retroflexion) and manner (fortition). It typically follows a lenition/fortition chain where the sibilant first retroflexes due to neighboring sounds (like [r], [i], or [u]) and then hardens into a stop.

examples.note: The RUKI rule in Proto-Indo-Aryan shifted sibilants to retroflexes, which then frequently hardened into stops in Middle Indo-Aryan Prakrits.
In many Scandinavian dialects, the retroflexion of the sibilant triggered by a preceding /r/ extends to the following stop, creating a complex retroflex cluster.
